title: Molar Mass
subtitle: Connecting moles to mass
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: explore the concept of the mole and relate this to Avogadro's constant to describe, calculate and manipulate numbers of particles, chemical amounts and masses
conduct a practical investigation to demonstrate and calculate the molar mass (mass of one mole) of an element and a compound
lessonIntent: Students can calculate molar mass and use it to move between grams and moles.
examSkill: Identify molar mass as the bridge between mass and moles in quantitative chemistry questions.

voiceover.text: Molar Mass. Year eleven Chemistry, Module two.

scenes.heading: We can count atoms, but can we weigh them?
scenes.body: The mole counts particles we cannot see. But chemists work in grams on a lab balance. We need a bridge between counting and weighing.
scenes.callout: Every counted particle has a mass.
scenes.caption: The mole counts invisible particles. Molar mass tells us how much they weigh.

voiceover.text: OK, here's the problem. Last lesson we got the mole locked in. It's a count of particles, six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three of them. But here's the thing. Chemists don't actually count atoms in a lab. They weigh them. And a dozen eggs and a dozen apples both contain twelve items, but they weigh totally different amounts. Same idea for moles. One mole of different substances has different masses. The mass per mole is what we call molar mass. And it's the bridge we've been missing.

scenes.heading: Molar mass is a bridge
scenes.body: It connects the particle world, where we count in moles, to the lab world, where we measure in grams. Without it, the mole is just a number.
scenes.callout: Always check the units: g mol⁻¹.
scenes.caption: Molar mass connects moles to grams.

voiceover.text: Molar mass is the bridge between the particle world and the lab world. On one side, moles, a count of particles. On the other side, grams, a mass we can actually measure on a balance. Molar mass connects them. It tells you how many grams one mole weighs. Without it, the mole is just an abstract number you can't really use.

scenes.heading: What is molar mass?
scenes.body: Molar mass is the mass of exactly one mole of a substance. Because the mole was defined using carbon-12, you can read molar mass straight from the periodic table. Think of molar mass like price per kilogram: it tells you the mass of one mole. Where the analogy breaks: price changes, but molar mass is fixed by the periodic table.

bullets.text: Mass of exactly one mole of a substance.
Measured in g mol⁻¹, grams per mole.
Defined using carbon-12, so it matches relative atomic mass.
Read it straight off the periodic table: H 1.008, C 12.01, O 16.00.

scenes.callout: One mole has a predictable mass.
scenes.caption: Molar mass equals relative atomic mass from the periodic table, measured in grams per mole.

voiceover.text: OK, so what is molar mass exactly? It's the mass of exactly one mole of a substance, in grams per mole. And here's the beautiful part. Because the mole was defined using carbon-twelve, the molar mass of any element equals its relative atomic mass straight from the periodic table. Take hydrogen. Atomic mass one-point-zero-zero-eight, so molar mass one-point-zero-zero-eight grams per mole. Same logic. Carbon, twelve-point-zero-one. Oxygen, sixteen-point-zero-zero. The periodic table gives you the answer directly. Think of molar mass like price per kilogram. It tells you the cost of one unit. Where the analogy breaks. Prices change. Molar mass is fixed by the periodic table.

scenes.confidenceCheck: Describe molar mass and read it directly from the periodic table for any element.

scenes.heading: Units matter
scenes.body: Molar mass is written in g mol⁻¹, grams per mole.
scenes.secondary: This unit is your safety check. When you see g mol⁻¹, you are talking about the mass of one mole, not the mass of your sample.
scenes.callout: Read g mol⁻¹ as grams per mole.
scenes.caption: Molar mass is measured in grams per mole, or g mol to the negative one.

voiceover.text: The unit for molar mass is grams per mole. Written as g, mol, to the minus one. This unit is your safety check. When you see those units, you know you're talking about the mass of one mole. Not the mass of your sample. Different thing. Molar mass links the atomic scale, where we count particles, to the lab scale, where we measure grams. It's the conversion factor that makes quantitative chemistry possible.

scenes.confidenceCheck: Identify g mol⁻¹ as the unit signalling 'mass per one mole'.

scenes.heading: From balance to moles
scenes.body: Chemists do not count molecules. They place a sample on a balance, read the mass in grams, and use molar mass to convert to moles.
scenes.caption: Lab balances measure mass. Molar mass turns grams into moles.

voiceover.text: Here's how this works in a real lab. Nobody counts out six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three molecules. That's impossible. Instead, you put a sample on a balance, read the mass in grams. Then use molar mass to turn that mass into moles. Mass on the balance gives you the number you measure. Molar mass turns that number into a chemical amount you can actually use.

scenes.heading: Mass, moles and molar mass
scenes.secondary: m is mass in grams. n is amount in moles. M is molar mass in grams per mole.

unitCancel.result: mol cancels → answer is in grams

scenes.caption: Use m equals n times M to calculate mass from moles and molar mass.

voiceover.text: OK, here's the formula that ties everything together. m equals n times capital M. Three quantities here. Lowercase m. That's the mass of your sample, in grams. Lowercase n. That's the amount of substance, in moles. And capital M. That's the molar mass, in grams per mole. Now watch the units. Moles times grams per mole. The mole units cancel. You're left with grams. That unit cancellation is your proof that the formula is set up correctly.

scenes.confidenceCheck: Apply m = n × M to convert between mass and amount of substance.

scenes.heading: Worked example
scenes.question: Calculate the molar mass of calcium dihydrogen phosphate, Ca(H₂PO₄)₂.
scenes.coachNote: Trap: the subscript 2 outside the bracket applies to every atom inside.
scenes.caption: Worked example: molar mass of Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ = 234.05 g mol⁻¹. Watch the bracket trap.

voiceover.text: Worked example. Calculate the molar mass of calcium dihydrogen phosphate, formula Ca H two P O four, with the H two P O four group in brackets and a subscript two outside. Half the class gets this wrong on the first try, so watch carefully. First, the calcium. Ca is forty-point-zero-eight grams per mole. Easy. Now the part inside the brackets. Two hydrogens at one-point-zero-zero-eight is two-point-zero-two. Plus phosphorus at thirty-point-nine-seven. Plus four oxygens at fifteen-point-nine-nine each, which is sixty-three-point-nine-nine. Total inside the bracket: ninety-six-point-nine-eight grams per mole. Now here's the trap. The subscript two outside the bracket means there are two of everything inside. So we multiply ninety-six-point-nine-eight by two. That gives one-hundred-ninety-three-point-nine-six. Add the calcium: forty-point-zero-eight plus one-hundred-ninety-three-point-nine-six equals two-hundred-thirty-four-point-zero-five grams per mole. If you got one-hundred-thirty-four, you forgot to multiply through the brackets. That's the most expensive mistake in this topic.

scenes.confidenceCheck: Calculate the molar mass of a compound, including those with brackets.

scenes.heading: Two costly mistakes
scenes.body: Mistake 1: confusing molar mass (M) with sample mass (m). M is a property of the substance, fixed by the periodic table. m is the mass of the sample you're holding.
scenes.secondary: Mistake 2: forgetting to multiply through brackets. In Ca(H₂PO₄)₂, the subscript 2 applies to every atom inside, not just the last.
scenes.callout: Pause at every bracket. Pause before every substitution.
scenes.caption: Two traps: confusing M and m; forgetting to multiply through brackets.

voiceover.text: Two mistakes that cost the most marks in this topic. First: confusing molar mass, capital M, with mass, lowercase m. Molar mass is a property of the substance, the mass of exactly one mole. It never changes for a given compound. Mass, lowercase m, is the mass of the sample you're holding. Before every question, ask: am I finding the mass of one mole, or the mass of my sample? Second, even more expensive: forgetting to multiply through brackets. In a formula like Ca H two P O four with a subscript two outside the bracket, that subscript applies to every atom inside the bracket, not just the last one. Miss this and your molar mass will be wrong by a factor that costs you full marks. Always pause when you see brackets and ask: how many of each atom do I actually have?

scenes.confidenceCheck: Distinguish molar mass (M) from sample mass (m) before substituting.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: How many moles are in 71.0 g of chlorine gas, Cl₂?
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: do I need M or m first?
scenes.caption: Find the molar mass of Cl₂ (remember the diatomic), then divide.

voiceover.text: Quick check. How many moles are in seventy-one-point-zero grams of chlorine gas? Pause and try this yourself. First, the molar mass of chlorine gas. Chlorine gas is Cl two, two atoms per molecule. So molar mass is two times thirty-five-point-four-five, which is seventy-point-nine grams per mole. If you wrote just thirty-five-point-four-five, you forgot the subscript two. Now rearrange the formula. m equals n times M, so n equals m divided by M. Seventy-one divided by seventy-point-nine is approximately one-point-zero-zero moles. Check the units: grams divided by grams per mole leaves moles. Correct.

scenes.confidenceCheck: Rearrange m = n × M to solve for any of the three quantities.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Before calculating: do I need M or m?
points: Molar mass is the mass of exactly one mole, measured in g mol⁻¹.
Read molar mass from the periodic table for elements. Add atomic masses for compounds.
Use m = n × M to convert between mass and moles.
Capital M and lowercase m are completely different, never confuse them.
Always check that units cancel to what you expect.
scenes.caption: Molar mass connects amount in moles to mass in grams.

voiceover.text: OK, let's lock in what we've covered. Five things to take away. First, molar mass is the mass of exactly one mole of a substance. Units, grams per mole. Next, you can read it straight from the periodic table for elements, or add atomic masses for compounds. Use m equals n times M to convert between mass and moles. Don't mix up capital M and lowercase m. Capital M is the molar mass, a fixed property of the substance. Lowercase m is just the mass of your sample. And always check that your units cancel to what you expect. That's the safety net.

inquiryQuestion: How are measurements made in chemistry?

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today: molar mass, the bridge between moles and grams. Let's get into it.
